hero.tagline: Indiana's small claims courts offer a streamlined process for resolving disputes up to $10,000, with informal procedures and quick resolutions.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Varies by county and number of defendants

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: Circuit or Superior Court (small claims docket)

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Appeal to Court of Appeals within 30 days

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the small claims limit. You must either waive the excess amount or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Indiana allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed, with a statute of limitations of 6 years for written contracts and 6 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, which they promised to repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails, texts, or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent $5,000 to a colleague who agreed to repay in a year, but they haven't made any payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Gather any written agreements, payment records, and communication regarding the loan.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $8,000 over two years but stopped making payments after six months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided consulting services worth $3,500, invoiced the client, but they haven't paid.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their store credit account and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present account statements and records of charges and payments.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $1,500 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property without paying.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any communication regarding the unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods worth $500 with a check that was returned for insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Send a 30-day demand letter before filing. Bring the bounced check and any related correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer liability for defaulted loan
claims.example: You co-signed a $7,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now the lender is seeking payment from you.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the loan agreement and any communication from the lender regarding the default.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Indiana provides avenues for consumers to seek redress for defective products and services, with a statute of limitations of 2 years for such claims.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a new refrigerator for $1,200 that stopped working after two months, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of defect. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $300 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks satisfactorily.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and photos of the unsatisfactory work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $800 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and now refuses to refund or re-repair.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Incomplete or poor-quality contractor work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $5,000 to renovate your bathroom, but the work was left unfinished and subpar.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from completion date. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of the incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You hired a DJ for your wedding for $1,500, but they never showed up and refuse to refund your payment.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from event date. Bring the service agreement, payment proof, and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a gym membership, but they continued to charge your account for three months totaling $300.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements showing the charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $7,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from purchase date. Bring the sales contract, payment proof, and any communication with the dealer.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product as 'guaranteed to work,' but it failed, causing you a loss of $500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from purchase date. Provide the advertisement, purchase receipt, and evidence of the product's failure.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Indiana allows claims for property damage with a statute of limitations of 2 years from the date of damage.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,000 in damages, and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor's actions damaged your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence during a storm, causing $1,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left your rental unit with $2,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the lease agreement, photos of the damage, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed landscaping worth $800.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your laptop with a repair shop, and they returned it with a cracked screen, refusing to take responsibility.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Bring the service agreement, photos of the damage, and any communication with the shop.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Indiana provides a 2-year statute of limitations for landlord-tenant disputes, including security deposit issues and uninhabitable conditions.

claims.name: Wrongful withholding of security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord refused to return your $1,200 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Failure to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without specifying the reasons.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and any correspondence regarding the deposit deductions.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, and the landlord refused to address them, forcing you to move out.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of condition. Provide photos, maintenance requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord retained personal property after move-out
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your furniture and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from move-out date. Bring the lease agreement, move-out notice, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your belongings.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from eviction date. Provide the lease agreement, eviction notice (if any), and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent after early termination
claims.example: You paid last month's rent in advance, but after terminating the lease early, the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed two months of rent totaling $2,400 and has since vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any communication regarding the unpaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent without proper notice
claims.example: Your tenant deducted $300 from rent for repairs they made without informing you beforehand.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from deduction date. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any communication regarding the repairs.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Indiana allows employees to file claims for unpaid wages and related issues, with a statute of limitations of 2 years for such claims.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $1,000 for hours worked but refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from wage due date. Provide pay stubs, timesheets, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $800.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from termination date. Bring employment records, resignation notice, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $2,500 in sales commissions, but your employer refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from commission due date. Provide sales records, commission agreements, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime last month, but your employer paid only regular wages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from overtime worked. Bring timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Indiana allows individuals to file claims for minor injuries and property disputes, with a statute of limitations of 2 years for such claims.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in a sprained ankle and $1,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your camera worth $600, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of ownership and any communication regarding the loan.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your bicycle worth $400 without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from theft date. Provide proof of ownership and any communication with the neighbor.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Indiana allows claims related to vehicle disputes, with a statute of limitations of 2 years for such claims.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $1,200 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and now refuses to refund or re-repair.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company charged you $300 for its return.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from tow date. Provide photos of the parking spot, tow receipt, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled in small claims court but have specific procedures and requirements distinct from other claims.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Small Claims Court with eviction procedures

exclusions.explanation: Small claims courts do not have jurisdiction over disputes involving the title to real estate.
Claims involving defamation require complex legal procedures and are not suitable for small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims involve specialized legal standards and are beyond the scope of small claims court.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are handled by specialized courts and are not within small claims jurisdiction.
Small claims courts do not have jurisdiction over claims against federal agencies or the federal government.
Claims for workers' compensation benefits are handled by the Indiana Workers' Compensation Board, not small claims court.
Class action lawsuits involve multiple plaintiffs and complex legal issues, making them unsuitable for small claims court.
Small claims courts cannot issue injunctions or orders for specific performance; they can only award monetary damages.
Bankruptcy proceedings are handled exclusively by federal bankruptcy courts and are not within the jurisdiction of small claims courts.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in Indiana small claims court; however, statutory penalties may be available for certain claims, such as bad checks.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees are generally not recoverable in Indiana small claims court unless there is a written agreement making the other party liable for such fees.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the Circuit or Superior Court

filing_options: County where the defendant resides or is employed
Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local court clerk for specific information.

service_fees.notes: Fee for each named defendant that is not a garnishee defendant.
Contact the local sheriff's office for specific fees.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court clerk for details.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, you must send a 30-day demand letter before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but it's advisable to request payment in writing before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 45 days of lease termination when withholding a security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims, but documenting all communications is beneficial.

forms.name: Notice of Claim
forms.description: Initiates the small claims lawsuit by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and the relief sought.

forms.name: Affidavit of Indigency
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to the plaintiff's financial inability to pay.

forms.name: Subpoena
forms.description: Orders a witness to appear in court or produce documents relevant to the case.

forms.name: Motion to Vacate Default Judgment
forms.description: Requests the court to set aside a default judgment entered due to a party's failure to appear.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of the intent to appeal the small claims judgment.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: The court clerk sends the summons and complaint to the defendant via certified mail.

methods.name: Personal service by sheriff
methods.description: A sheriff or deputy personally delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent of the corporation or an officer; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Indiana rules and the rules of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the scheduled hearing to avoid default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must present evidence to support the claim and amount sought.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the denial within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to a higher court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; parties present evidence and testimony directly to the judge.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide assistance upon request.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few days
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts offer mediation services; check with the local court for availability.

appeals.type_notes: Appeals are based on the record of the small claims court; new evidence is typically not considered.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed, but it is advisable to first file a motion to vacate in the small claims court.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on unpaid judgments at a rate of 8% per annum.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a motion for proceedings supplemental, serve the employer with a garnishment order, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a court order, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county recorder's office to create a lien on the debtor's real property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, have the sheriff seize debtor's non-exempt personal property, and sell it at public auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 10-year period expires; renewal extends the judgment for another 10 years.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can represent themselves in Indiana small claims court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must file a Certificate of Assumed Business Name with the county recorder.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors can send a designated employee to represent them in small claims court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts in court; ensure proper licensing before filing a claim.

county_variations.detail: Marion County operates separate township small claims courts with jurisdiction over cases up to $8,000.
Lake County offers e-filing for small claims cases through the Indiana E-Filing System.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect venue selection
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing in the wrong venue can lead to dismissal or transfer of your case, causing delays and additional costs.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure you file in the correct county based on the defendant's residence, employment, or where the incident occurred.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the monetary limit
pitfalls.what_happens: Claims exceeding $10,000 are not eligible for small claims court and must be filed in a higher court.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: If your claim exceeds the limit, consider waiving the excess amount or filing in the appropriate higher court.

pitfalls.title: Failure to serve defendant properly
pitfalls.what_happens: Improper service can result in delays or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Indiana's rules for service of process carefully, using certified mail, sheriff, or a private process server as appropriate.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be aware of the specific statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the required timeframe.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can lead to losing your case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness information to support your claim before filing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you are unprepared, you could lose and owe money.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to address any counterclaims by gathering evidence and understanding the defendant's potential arguments.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims court monetary limit
recent_changes.description: The jurisdictional limit for small claims court increased from $6,000 to $10,000, allowing for higher-value disputes to be resolved in small claims court.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Indiana small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves. However, you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Most small claims cases in Indiana are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing, depending on the court's schedule and the complexity of the case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: As of 2026, the maximum amount you can sue for in Indiana small claims court is $10,000, not including interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the scheduled hearing, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor. You may still need to present evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims court decision to the Indiana Court of Appeals within 30 days of the judgment.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Indiana is valid for 10 years and can be renewed for an additional 10 years if necessary.

state_specific_notes: Indiana allows for wage garnishment as a collection method, with certain exemptions in place.
Small claims courts in Indiana do not handle cases involving title to real estate.
Eviction cases are handled in small claims court but have specific procedures distinct from other claims.
Indiana's small claims courts are designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves, with informal procedures.
The jurisdictional limit for small claims court in Indiana is $10,000 as of 2026.

interesting_findings: Indiana's small claims courts emphasize speedy and informal resolution of disputes, often resulting in same-day judgments.
Marion County operates separate township small claims courts with jurisdiction over cases up to $8,000, differing from the rest of the state.
Indiana law requires a 30-day demand letter before filing a bad check claim in small claims court.
Businesses can represent themselves in Indiana small claims court without an attorney, which is not permitted in all states.
The statute of limitations for written and oral contracts in Indiana is 6 years, longer than in many other states.
